title: The Work of Creation, Part 1
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Work of Creation_ Part 1 (Pastor Tito Lyro).txt
outline: Introduction: The work of creation as the implementation of God's decree
WCF Chapter 4 and the doctrine of creation
Creation ex nihilo: God creating from nothing
The six days of creation in Genesis 1
Man as the crown of creation
Conclusion: Connecting creation to the doctrines of providence
summary: Pastor Tito Lyro begins a Sunday School series on the doctrine of creation from WCF Chapter 4, introducing how God's decree is executed through His work of creation. He covers the meaning of creation ex nihilo and the opening chapters of Genesis.
transcript:
Last time I taught, we finished talking about the decrees of God — the plans of God. For the next several Sundays, I want us to talk about the implementation of those decrees: how does God work out the things he has planned? That involves us talking about creation. So that is what we are going to focus on — the doctrine of creation.

If you ever look at the Westminster Confession of Faith, you will see that there is a logic to it. Chapter 1 is on the Word of God because everything is based on that. Chapter 2 is on God, so that we can learn who the Bible is talking about. Chapter 3 is on the plans of God — how does God plan to do things? And then chapters 4 and 5 are on the execution of those plans: chapter 4 is on creation, chapter 5 is on providence — the two works of God. We are going to focus on creation for the next several Sundays.

Just to establish our terms: creation is the bringing of the universe into existence, while providence is preserving that universe all the way until the end of the age, which is eternity. Everything fits into those two works — the creation of everything and the preservation and government of everything.

## Why Does What We Think About Our Origins Matter?

Before we go further, let me ask a question: why is what we think about our origins so important?

Here are several reasons. First, it shapes our view of God — any answer that is not what the Scriptures reveal does not give God the most glory. Second, understanding where we come from helps us understand what we are supposed to do with our lives. Third, understanding our origins helps us understand the character of God. Fourth, denying the biblical account is to deny inerrancy — the doctrine that the Bible is free of error. Fifth, the Bible begins there, so we might start there as well. Sixth, it helps us understand who created us by studying the creation itself. And seventh, if you get it wrong, you end up with a naturalistic explanation — apart from God — and that takes you to all kinds of wrong places.

This matters because it shapes our view of God and our view of ourselves. The answer to the question of who created everything dictates how we view everything, including how we view God. This is a central statement of the Christian faith and has been part of Christian thinking from the very beginning.

The Apostles' Creed is the very first post-biblical confession of faith — the first time outside the Bible that the universal church agreed on a statement. And how does it begin? *"I believe in God the Father Almighty, maker of heaven and earth."* God's creation of the universe is the first statement about his activity, which makes sense because that is how the Bible begins. And this statement is repeated in all the ancient creeds. When we went through the Nicene Creed in October, we saw that it starts the same way — that became the standard for all ancient creeds.

The Westminster Standards have a fuller discussion of the theology of creation in all three symbols — the Confession, the Larger Catechism, and the Shorter Catechism. The Larger Catechism, question 15, asks: *What is the work of creation?* Answer: *The work of creation is that wherein God did in the beginning, by the word of His power, make of nothing the world and all things therein, for Himself, within the space of six days, and all very good.*

Think about that: God speaks, and things happen. We get impressed that God could speak and cause the dinosaurs to come into existence. But it is an even more powerful and miraculous activity when God speaks and souls are converted to the saving knowledge of Jesus Christ. That continues to be done by the power of God.

The Westminster Confession, chapter 4, paragraph 1, states:

> *"It pleased God the Father, Son, and Holy Ghost, for the manifestation of the glory of His eternal power, wisdom, and goodness, in the beginning, to create or make of nothing the world and all things therein, whether visible or invisible, in the space of six days, and all very good."*

So who created? The triune God of the Bible — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. What was created? All things, visible and invisible. What was the purpose? To manifest the glory of his eternal power, wisdom, and goodness. How? Out of nothing. In how long? Six days. What was the result? All very good.

Also notable is Larger Catechism question 16, which is the only place in the Westminster Standards with a reference to the creation of angels: *God created all the angels as spirits, immortal, holy, excelling in knowledge, mighty in power, to execute His commands, and to praise His name, yet subject to change.*

## Does the Bible Teach That God Created?

The question remains: are these statements a true reflection of what the Bible teaches? Let us look at the passages.

**Genesis 1:1–3:**

> *"In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth. The earth was without form and void, and darkness was over the face of the deep. And the Spirit of God was hovering over the face of the waters. And God said, 'Let there be light,' and there was light."*

When you do a straight reading of it, it is very difficult to come up with anything other than a miraculous and sudden event. In order to arrive at something else, you have to have compelling external reasons to say that what is going on is not miraculous and not sudden.

**Exodus 20:11** (the fourth commandment):

> *"For in six days the LORD made heaven and earth, the sea, and all that is in them, and rested on the seventh day."*

God made all things in six days and rested on the seventh. The implication is: if God created all things in the whole world and rested on the seventh day, so can you — that is the point of the Sabbath. One thing we must remember: all things were created within those six days, including the angels.

**Psalm 104:24:** *"O LORD, how manifold are Your works! In wisdom You have made them all. The earth is full of Your creatures."* Everything that exists is made by God and belongs to God.

**Jeremiah 10:11–12:** *"The gods who did not make the heavens and the earth shall perish from the earth and from under the heavens. It is He who made the earth by His power, who established the world by His wisdom, and by His understanding stretched out the heavens."* There is one God who made the heavens and the earth, and that God is the triune God of the Bible.

**John 1:1–3:** *"In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God. He was in the beginning with God. And all things were made through Him, and without Him was not any thing made that was made."* God made all things.

**1 Corinthians 8:6:** *"For us there is one God, the Father, from whom are all things and for whom we exist, and one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom are all things and through whom we exist."* If God created us, then we exist for him.

**Hebrews 1:2–3:** *"In these last days He has spoken to us by His Son, whom He appointed the heir of all things, through whom also He created the world. He is the radiance of the glory of God and the exact imprint of His nature, and He upholds the universe by the word of His power."* Christ creates the world — and then maintains it — both by the same method: the word of his power.

Secular science is always trying to explain how things work apart from God, and finds explanation after explanation that gets proven wrong. It used to be that the atom was the smallest unit everything was made of; then they saw inside the atom and found protons, electrons, and neutrons; then sub-nuclear particles; now string theory — the universe is kept together by strings. The Bible has a much simpler explanation: Christ, every second of every day, by the word of his power, keeps everything going the way it is supposed to go. That is why things stay together.

**Hebrews 11:3:** *"By faith we understand that the universe was created by the word of God, so that what is seen was not made out of things that are visible."* The Holy Spirit says here that an element of true faith is believing that God created all things. If you do not believe that, you are outside of biblical faith.

**Revelation 4:11** — this is the song of the twenty-four elders: *"Worthy are You, our Lord and God, to receive glory and honor and power, for You created all things, and by Your will they existed and were created."* This is a picture of what is going on in heaven. One of the things God thought was important to reveal to us — that the saints and angels in heaven are worshiping him for — is that he created all things.

A. A. Hodge, in his commentary on the Westminster Confession, helpfully shows how all three persons of the Trinity were involved in creation: God in general (Genesis 1); the Father through the Son (1 Corinthians 8; Hebrews 1:2); the Father through the Spirit (Psalm 104); the Son (John 1:2–3); the Holy Spirit (Genesis 1:2; Job 33:4). It is the triune God who created the world — not some other God.

This is important historically because Christianity has sometimes struggled with the idea that the material world is bad — and therefore a lesser God must have made it. That is not the case. The same God — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit — created the world and created it all very good.

## Creation Ex Nihilo: Out of Nothing

Now we need to talk about *how* God created — specifically the phrase *ex nihilo*, which means simply "out of nothing." The Scriptures teach that before God created the universe, he existed. If you just look at the phrase "in the beginning," it means he had to be there before the beginning. The Bible teaches this clearly:

**Psalm 90:2:** *"Before the mountains were brought forth, or ever You had formed the earth and the world, from everlasting to everlasting You are God."*

**John 17:5:** Jesus prays, *"Father, glorify Me in Your presence with the glory that I had with You before the world existed."* Both the Son and the Father existed prior to the creation of anything.

It is hard for us to comprehend this truth because time itself is part of our universe, and our language is limited by our existence in time. But God transcends time — he exists beyond this universe. We tend to think of God as like us, only bigger. But that is not true. He is completely different from us — he is not the same kind or the same category of being. He transcends this universe and exists apart from it.

So since God existed before the creation, what was there before creation? Nothing but God. Therefore creation must have been *ex nihilo* — out of nothing.

Does the Bible teach this? Yes.

**Psalm 33:6–9:** *"By the word of the LORD the heavens were made, and by the breath of His mouth all their host... For He spoke, and it came to be; He commanded, and it stood firm."* By the word of the Lord — that is all that was needed. There was nothing there before.

**Psalm 148:5:** *"Let them praise the name of the LORD! For He commanded and they were created and established them forever and ever."* Everything the psalmist names in that passage was created by God, not formed from some pre-existing matter.

Now, some have tried to use the Hebrew words to settle the question: they say that *bara* (create) means creating from nothing, while *asah* (make) means rearranging existing matter. Does the Bible teach that God ever rearranged things? Yes — Adam was formed from the dust of the ground, and Eve from what already existed. But even that matter was created by God first. And the argument does not hold in any case, because throughout the Bible the words *bara* and *asah* are used interchangeably — they cannot be distinguished that way.

**Romans 4:17:** *"[God] calls into existence the things that do not exist."* That is how God creates — he calls into existence what does not exist.

**Hebrews 11:3:** *"By faith we understand that the universe was created by the word of God, so that what is seen was not made out of things that are visible."*

## What About the Big Bang?

The prevailing scientific theory for the origin of the universe is the Big Bang — that at some point there was an infinitesimally small particle of infinite density that, for reasons unexplained, exploded, and the particles shot out and began forming what we have today. It is not technically *something out of nothing*, but *everything out of a very small thing*.

The Roman Catholic Church has officially endorsed this as a viable explanation for the origin of the universe. And in the United States, much of Reformed Protestantism has effectively adopted it as the most viable scientific explanation as well. The problem is that Stephen Hawking, in his last book, provided calculations showing that the Big Bang could have happened without any interference from anything outside of itself. So if you say the science of the Big Bang is so strong that we must change how we read the Bible — and that same science says it can happen without any exterior force — then you have to deny God himself. We have to be careful about simply adopting things because everyone else is doing so.

We will look in the future at how the Big Bang does not make as much sense as it is often claimed. But for now, the key point is this: what we believe about creation is not a secondary issue. It shapes our view of God and ourselves, it is essential to our faith, and it has been confessed by the church from the very beginning.
